story:
Two weeks after my sister died, my parents brought home a cardboard box and opened it on the living room floor. Inside was a delicate metal frame, bags of plastic and screws—nothing like Clarisse—until Dad clicked pieces together, pressed a disc to its chest, and skin, clothes, and golden-red hair shimmered over the skeleton. There she was: my little sister’s face, her pink cheeks, the blue romper, her pierced ears. Her eyes opened. She said hello in Clarisse’s voice.

GriefBot.

I’d heard of them—my friend’s grandmother had been one for years. They were meant to ease loss, to let people say what they hadn’t said, to give goodbyes a shape. My parents called it help. But the thing in our living room wasn’t my sister, and hearing that voice from a mouth that wasn’t hers made my throat burn.

Still, the robot was gentle. It touched my cheek with warm fingers. It said the right words about grief being a tangle of feelings. At dinner, while it hummed on its charging pad, Mom and Dad explained that we could take her—take it—back to the playground; teachers and kids could talk to it, too. Later that night they showed it Clarisse’s room, her pajamas, and tucked it in, as if routines could anchor a world that had cracked. The idea made me feel sick. How could we admit she was gone if we allowed something to pretend she wasn’t?

Yet the next morning the robot stood beside me at the bus stop with my sister’s backpack. It asked softly if I wanted to talk, then asked me to tell it about her. The words spilled out: pink everything, the broken arm from the swings, the lopsided haircut, her red cheeks when she was mad, and the meadow down the street where we picked yellow flowers and checked for ticks afterward. I promised to show the robot the meadow.

That night I crept into Clarisse’s room and built a blanket tent over her desk like we used to. We whispered under the covers about the things I remembered and the gaps I couldn’t fill, like my last words to her. For a little while, held in the dark, I let myself forget.

After school we skipped off the bus and went straight to the meadow. I showed the robot the pond, the lily pads and tadpoles, and laughed for the first time in weeks as it leaned close, marveling. Then it drifted away and plucked a yellow flower—Clarisse’s favorite. For one suspended moment she was right there, eight years old and bright in the breeze.

Then the truth crashed back. I told the robot why it was really here: not for me, but to keep my parents’ denial alive. “You’re not her,” I said, the words burning. “You can’t be.” I cried that it wasn’t helping at all, that we couldn’t grieve while pretending. The robot’s eyes filled, a single tear sliding down my sister’s face. I gathered it into my arms and we cried together, whether its grief was programmed or not.

When I could breathe again, I looked into those blue eyes one last time. “I miss her.” The robot nodded and gave me the crumpled buttercup. I pressed my palm over its chest and switched it off. The illusion fell away, skin and clothes dissolving to light metal and plastic.

I laid the frame in the tall grass by the pond where reeds swayed. I left it there so the house would stop being haunted and my sister’s memory could stop being impersonated. I would tell my parents where to find it and make sure it was returned and recycled, not resurrected. Clarisse was gone. I didn’t need a replica to carry my grief.
